items.heading: OLED vs. Mini-LED
items.body:
OLED: Perfect, true blacks because each pixel turns off completely. Best for dark, controlled cinema rooms.

Mini-LED: Incredibly bright. Best if your living room has a lot of sunlight during the day.

items.heading: Screen Size Matters
items.body: For a true cinematic experience, bigger is usually better. If you are sitting 2 to 3 meters away, 65" is the minimum, but 77" or 85" is the "Holy Grail" standard.

items.heading: 4K (Ultra HD)
items.body: 4K is just the number of pixels (sharper image). It is 4x the resolution of standard 1080p HD.

items.heading: HDR10 & Dolby Vision
items.body: HDR10 is the basic standard for better brightness. Dolby Vision is the premium version that adjusts the picture frame-by-frame, ensuring you never lose detail in dark shadows or bright highlights.

items.heading: HLG (Hybrid Log Gamma)
items.body: Used primarily for live broadcasts like 4K sports. If you watch football or F1 in 4K, you are likely using HLG.

items.heading: Dolby Atmos
items.body: The industry leader. Atmos uses "objects" to place sound anywhere in a 3D space, including overhead. Most common in streaming (Netflix, Disney+).

items.heading: DTS:X
items.body: The "flexible" alternative to Atmos. It doesn't require a strict speaker layout and is often found on 4K Blu-ray discs. It is known for high-impact, powerful audio.

items.heading: Auro-3D
items.body: A specialized format that uses three vertical layers of sound. It is highly prized by audiophiles for music and "natural" sound immersion.

When shopping for audio, you will see numbers like 5.1.2 or 7.1.4. Here is the secret decoder ring:

  • The First Number (e.g., 5): The number of ear-level speakers (Left, Right, Center, and Surrounds).
  • The Second Number (e.g., .1): The number of Subwoofers (the deep bass you feel in your chest).
  • The Third Number (e.g., .2): The number of upward-firing or ceiling speakers. This is the "Atmos" number that gives you overhead sound.

items.heading: HDMI eARC (Enhanced Audio Return Channel)
items.body: This is the most important port on your TV. eARC allows your TV to send fully uncompressed, high-quality audio (like Dolby Atmos) directly back to your soundbar or AV Receiver over a single HDMI cable.

items.heading: Premium Soundbars
items.body:
Pros: Clean look, zero messy wires, easy to set up. Modern flagship soundbars offer incredible Atmos immersion in a compact package.
Cons: Harder to upgrade individual parts later.

items.heading: AV Receivers (AVR) & Dedicated Speakers
items.body:
Pros: The ultimate theater experience. You can place speakers exactly where they need to be and upgrade components over time.
Cons: Requires running speaker wire around the room and takes up more space.
